item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The opening of the article kills it for me. The attempted one-upmanship with that opening speech about this being the REAL DEAL feels hollow when we have so many other SCPs basically proclaiming the same thing. At some point 'biggest baddest world ending unstoppable thing emerges' is just Tuesday at the Foundation.

The last couple of lines feel a bit overmuch too. A good stinger at the end of an article is a great sendoff, but having a bunch of stingers all in a row diminishes them individually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to ask, is this the final push of yaldabaoth that you mentioned. In any case this remains one of the best things I’ve seen on this site, such sheer anticipation and world building.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe the scrambling letters represent tiamat hacking the document to add her comment or something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have only two questions after reading this:

  1. Is there a meaning behind the rapidly changing sequence of numbers and letters towards the end of the article?
  2. Did you forget to explain what the designation "SCP-3895-Ω" refers to? Compare it to "SCP-3895-Prime", which you explain as being the tentative designation of the mass of -3895 subordinate instances that is "gestating" in preparation for the scip's "primary manifestation".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In case you're still looking for the "series", http://www.scp-wiki.net/cadavercommander . Start at "The Burden" and go from there. The first 3 on the page aren't really related.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, Cadavar. I was reading down the list of "Top New Pages" and you're just churning out hit after hit. I don't think I've even started on the stuff leading up to this one. At this rate you're going to end up breaking the sound barrier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for classic myth stuff, and this set of SCPs does it very well.

+ My take on this
- Close

IMO, the view that this SCP is trying to hard to be super deadly is kind of missing the point. What seems important here is what 3895-Ω (and 3894-Alpha) represent. In so far as 3895-Ω is Tiamat, and represents what Tiamat represents, it is unquestionably the single most dangerous anomaly in any canon that contains it (with the posible exception of an accepted 001, though I agree with TL333s that this feels like a 001 itself), not simply because of the mortal danger faced by humanity, but also because it is everything the Foundation stands against. This thing is Tiamat, mother of monsters, dragon of chaos, the antagonist of the oldest Chaoskampf we have on record. Narritively speaking, of course she would be the greatest antagonist to the Foundation, who's primary objective is maintaining consensus reality.

This really is classic stuff, and classics are classic for a reason. Yes, on one level of analysis it's a big complicated monster that's gonna kill everyone if the Foundation doesn't stop it, but there's a lot more to it, even setting aside the high quality of writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright I reread it after coming to my own conclusion on the series and I genuinely enjoyed this although I was expecting a jumpscare near the end.

+1

BTW, why is this not an Apollyon? It definitely seems like it would be

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this well-written? Absolutely. Phenomenal writing, for sure.

That said, I find this hard to enjoy. I feel that this SCP (like so many others) are just trying so damn hard to seem important, to be the biggest and baddest of all SCP's, and to end the world. SCP-990 is a dream man who warns people about the end of days, SCP-1678 is an underground city built for the end of days, SCP-2399 is an enormous destroyer on Jupiter that can bring about the end of days, along with countless other SCP's entirely premised on the end of days - many even directly causing the end. At this point, the concept in-and-of-itself deserves it's own full canon again considering how overused it has become.

I understand and respect that this is the amalgamation of your works, but the fact that it led to the same place as so many other SCP's cripples my ability to enjoy it. We've heard about Lifted Veil protocol and the Foundation going public (there's even a canon for it), we've heard about how *this* new SCP is the biggest-and-baddest threat out there (many times in many ways now), we've heard about our reality falling apart at the seams with the Foundation seemingly hopeless to stop it, and we've heard the dramatic lines all essentially saying, "This is it, we can't go back to being scared cavemen, either we win or shit's fucked."

Won't upvote for the above-stated issues, won't downvote because the writing itself is still remarkable. In the end, it all just seems to come to above-average writing with a below-average subject matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I should read this in some order and I'm at the end of a series or something

If so, which one should I read first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey dude? I think you're missing a couple hundred upvotes. Seriously, this series is awesome.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially down voted, because I was disappointed with the pay off from the previous articles, which I thought were stellar. I also don't like SCPs with notes that might as well say "this SCP is the biggest threat, forget the rest". I'm also not a fan of the SCP that came before this one, but I'll probably explain that on it's respective page.

After thinking it over, I removed my down vote, because that wasn't really fair of me. Even if I'm not satisfied, it's still a very well written SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To continue my comment from 3894,

This, on the other hand, is pretty stellar. It's got the same kind of shape as 3894, narratively speaking, but you've very effectively amped up the relative danger threshold in a natural way (albeit perhaps beating the reader over the head with "this scary and kill!" in the first note), and the imminent collapse of humanity is palpable. I really love the procedures. I will agree with Kakroom that the final few lines aren't quite as effective as "We will not reenter the womb", but this upvote is anything but tentative.

Though I'm surprised you didn't go for a 001 with this. It's got that feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm upvoting in spite of the final bits. They feel like individual wham lines, each of which is imo marginally less effective than 'we will not reenter the womb,' which I'd be willing to accept based off of its effectiveness despite it being a critical breach of tone, except you repeat it like four more times without explanation. You even include quotes from the entity, I guess? at the end, and it all just feels dissatisfactory for all the other effort you've put in.

I like the idea of a bunch of Doomguys attempting to fight off a Babylonian dragon goddess, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sheer logistics to suspend my disbelief, partnered with how dense this is on an events level makes this near impossible for me to enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been a very long time since anything on this site gave the raw sense of anticipation and excitement this does.

thaumonuclear

So beautiful I shed a tear.

item.title: ive really gone and done it now
item.content:encoded:
my ninth item, to consume and destroy my eighth. and everything else.

a kind of finale to my ill-advised ramblings so far.

an enormous thank you to everyone in chat and elsewhere that helped me with this. you know who you are.

and thank you, reader, for sticking with me until now. ♥
